---
title: "From pixels to patterns — results and visualisation"
author: "Emma Belaud"
format:
  html:
    css: css/styles.css  
    embed-resources: true
    code-tools: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 4
execute: 
  warning: false
  message: false
  echo: false
---

# Overview

## Role in the pipeline

This script is the **final step** of the SEM pipeline. It loads the path coefficient database produced by `check_models_performance.qmd` and produces three analytical sections:

| Section | Question | Output |
|----|----|----|
| **Environmental forcing** | How strongly and consistently do microclimate and root dynamics drive fauna across management types? | Distribution plots of standardised path coefficients and R² |
| **Deterministic vs. stochastic processes** | Are observed causal effects statistically distinguishable from random noise? | Z-score distributions relative to the permutation null |
| **Temporal stability** | Do the strengths of causal paths fluctuate over time, or remain steady? | Coefficient of variation (CV) per path and position |
| **Spatial synchrony** | Are the same causal rules operating simultaneously in both management zones? | Pearson correlation and rolling correlation of path coefficients (A vs. C) |

## Analytical design

Two management positions are contrasted throughout:

| Code  | Description                                             |
|-------|---------------------------------------------------------|
| **A** | Unmanaged system — tree cover and herbaceous vegetation |
| **C** | Managed system — cultivated cover                       |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 1. Setup

```{r setup}
invisible(Sys.setlocale("LC_TIME", "C"))
dir.create("../output", showWarnings = FALSE)

# ── Libraries ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
libs <- c(
  "tidyverse",    # Data wrangling and visualisation
  "lubridate",    # Date/time manipulation
  "ade4",         # Multivariate analysis
  "piecewiseSEM", # Piecewise SEM
  "performance",  # Model performance metrics
  "patchwork",    # Multi-panel figure composition
  "slider",       # Rolling window calculations
  "kableExtra",   # Enhanced HTML tables
  "phylopath",    # Phylogenetic path analysis utilities
  "e1071",        # Skewness / kurtosis
  "nlme",         # Mixed models with correlation structures
  "zoo",          # Time series tools
  "ggdist"        # Distribution visualisation (stat_halfeye, stat_dots)
)
invisible(lapply(libs, require, character.only = TRUE))

# ── Metadata ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
microclimate_index <- readLines("../output/microclimate_index.txt")
fauna_vars         <- readLines("../output/fauna_vars.txt")

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# VISUAL IDENTITY
# All colour palettes and the shared ggplot theme are centralised here.
# Any future style change requires editing only this block.
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# ── Master colour palette ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Earthy greens → warm terracottas, reflecting the soil-to-fauna causal gradient
SEM_COLORS <- list(

  # One colour per directed path (ordered abiotic → biotic)
  path = c(
    "#c2cab2",   # microclimate_1 → root  (light sage)
    "#555d45",   # microclimate_2 → root  (dark olive)
    "#dea88b",   # microclimate_1 → fauna (warm sand)
    "#d1855c",   # microclimate_2 → fauna (terracotta)
    "#955f42"    # root           → fauna (deep umber)
  ),

  # Response variables
  response = c(root = "#94a378", fauna = "#d1855c"),

  # Management positions
  position = c(A = "#555d45", C = "#d1855c"),

  # Reference lines
  null_line   = "firebrick",   # ±1.96 threshold lines
  zero_line   = "black",       # β = 0 reference
  segment     = "grey70",      # lollipop stems
  sync_anchor = "#385438",     # r = 0 reference in synchrony plots

  # Diverging palette for the synchrony gradient (negative → zero → positive)
  sync_low  = "#B2182B",
  sync_mid  = "#F7F7F7",
  sync_high = "#2166AC"
)

# Named vectors for scale_fill/colour_manual calls
path_palette     <- setNames(SEM_COLORS$path, NULL)   # assigned after path_levels
response_palette <- SEM_COLORS$response
position_palette <- SEM_COLORS$position

# ── Path-level ordering ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Paths are ordered from purely abiotic (microclimate → root) to
# biotic-mediated (root → fauna) to reflect the causal hierarchy
path_levels <- tibble(
  path_order = 1:5,
  path_id = c(
    paste0(microclimate_index[1], " -> root"),
    paste0(microclimate_index[2], " -> root"),
    paste0(microclimate_index[1], " -> fauna"),
    paste0(microclimate_index[2], " -> fauna"),
    "root -> fauna"
  )
)

path_palette <- setNames(SEM_COLORS$path, path_levels$path_id)

# ── Facet labellers ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Defined once so any change to position labels propagates to all figures
position_labeller <- c(A = "Unmanaged system", C = "Managed system")

# ── Shared ggplot theme ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
#' Base ggplot theme for all SEM figures
#'
#' Extends theme_minimal() with project-specific defaults:
#' bordered panels, suppressed minor gridlines, bold strip labels,
#' and configurable legend position. Pass additional theme() calls
#' after this function to override individual elements per plot.
#'
#' @param base_size  Integer. Base font size (default: 11).
#' @param legend_pos Character. Passed to legend.position (default: "none").
#' @return A ggplot2 theme object.
theme_sem <- function(base_size = 11, legend_pos = "none") {
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_size) %+replace%
    theme(
      # Panel
      panel.border     = element_rect(color = "black", fill = NA, linewidth = 0.5),
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey92", linewidth = 0.3),

      # Strip labels (facet headers)
      strip.text       = element_text(size = base_size - 1, face = "bold"),
      strip.background = element_blank(),

      # Axes
      axis.title       = element_text(size = base_size - 1),
      axis.text        = element_text(size = base_size - 2),

      # Title hierarchy
      plot.title    = element_text(size = base_size, face = "bold", hjust = 0,
                                   margin = margin(b = 4)),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(size = base_size - 1, color = "grey40",
                                   hjust = 0, margin = margin(b = 6)),
      plot.caption  = element_text(size = base_size - 2, color = "grey50",
                                   hjust = 0, margin = margin(t = 6)),

      # Legend
      legend.position = legend_pos,
      legend.title    = element_text(size = base_size - 1, face = "bold"),
      legend.text     = element_text(size = base_size - 2),

      # Margins
      plot.margin = margin(8, 8, 8, 8)
    )
}

# ── SEM results database ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
data_sem_results <- read.csv(
  "../output/SEM_results_database.csv",
  na.strings = c("", "NA")
) %>%
  mutate(taxon = if_else(taxon == "fauna", "null model", taxon)) %>%
  left_join(path_levels, by = "path_id")%>%
  # Replace internal microclimate aliases with their actual index names
  mutate(
    predictor = case_when(
      predictor == "microclimate_1" ~ microclimate_index[1],
      predictor == "microclimate_2" ~ microclimate_index[2],
      TRUE ~ predictor
    ),
    path_id = str_replace(path_id, "microclimate_1", microclimate_index[1]),
    path_id = str_replace(path_id, "microclimate_2", microclimate_index[2])
  )
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 2. Environmental forcing across system types

> How strongly and consistently do microclimate and root dynamics drive fauna across management types?

**Directed acyclic graph (DAG) — causal structure**

![](images/DAG.png)

```{r prep-plot-data}
data_plot <- data_sem_results %>%
  filter(type == "observed")  %>%
  # Remove extreme outliers: retain values within Q1 ± 1.5 × IQR
  filter(
    std_estimate >= quantile(std_estimate, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE) -
                    1.5 * IQR(std_estimate, na.rm = TRUE),
    std_estimate <= quantile(std_estimate, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE) +
                    1.5 * IQR(std_estimate, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  mutate(path_id = factor(path_id, levels = path_levels$path_id))

# Significance rate: proportion of windows yielding p < 0.05 per path × position
pval_summary <- data_plot %>%
  group_by(path_id, position) %>%
  summarise(
    n_total  = n(),
    n_sig    = sum(p_value < 0.05, na.rm = TRUE),
    prop_sig = (n_sig / n_total) * 100,
    .groups  = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(label = paste0("Sig. rate:\n ", round(prop_sig, 1), "%"))
```

### 2.1 Path coefficient distributions (significant paths only)

```{r plot-estimates}
#| fig-cap: >
#|   Raincloud plots of standardised SEM path coefficients (β) across rolling
#|   time windows. Only windows with p < 0.05 are shown. Dots represent
#|   individual window estimates (hexagonal binning); the shaded half-eye shows
#|   the kernel density; the box summarises the IQR and median.
#|   'Sig. rate' = proportion of all windows (including non-significant) yielding
#|   p < 0.05 for that path. Outliers beyond Q1 ± 1.5 × IQR are excluded.
#| fig-alt: >
#|   Faceted raincloud plots showing the distribution of path coefficients
#|   for five causal pathways, split by management position.
#| fig-width:  14
#| fig-height: 5

p_estimates <- data_plot %>%
  #filter(p_value < 0.05) %>%
  ggplot(aes(std_estimate, fill = path_id)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, color = SEM_COLORS$zero_line, linewidth = 0.3) +
  stat_dots(
    side = "top", justification = -0.1,
    binwidth = unit(c(0.001, 0.02), "npc"),
    color = "darkgrey", fill = "darkgrey", layout = "hex"
  ) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 1, width = 0.5, justification = -0.1, .width = 0, alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.12, outliers = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = path_palette) +
  facet_grid(
    position ~ path_id,
    labeller = labeller(
      position = position_labeller,
      path_id  = label_wrap_gen(width = 15)
    )
  ) +
  geom_text(
    data        = pval_summary,
    aes(x = Inf, y = Inf, label = label),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    hjust = 1.1, vjust = 1.5, size = 3, fontface = "italic"
  ) +
  guides(fill = "none", color = "none") +
  labs(
    title    = "Distribution of significant environmental forcing effects",
    subtitle = "Standardised path coefficients (p < 0.05) across management types and causal paths",
    x        = "Standardised path coefficient (β)",
    y        = NULL
  ) +
  theme_sem()

p_estimates
```

### 2.2 Model explanatory power (conditional R²)

```{r plot-r2}
#| fig-cap: >
#|   Distribution of conditional R² values across rolling windows for the root
#|   and fauna sub-models, split by management position. Conditional R² captures
#|   variance explained by both fixed effects (microclimate, root) and random
#|   effects (orientation / depth). Higher values indicate that the full model
#|   structure, including spatial grouping, accounts for a large fraction of
#|   observed variability.
#| fig-alt: >
#|   Faceted half-eye plots showing conditional R² distributions for root and
#|   fauna models in unmanaged and managed systems.
#| fig-width:  6
#| fig-height: 5

p_r2 <- data_plot %>%
  mutate(response = factor(response, levels = c("root", "fauna"))) %>%
  ggplot(aes(r2_cond, fill = response)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 1, width = 0.5, justification = -0.1, .width = 0, alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.12, outliers = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = response_palette) +
  facet_grid(
    position ~ response,
    labeller = labeller(position = position_labeller),
    scales   = "free"
  ) +
  guides(fill = "none", color = "none") +
  labs(
    title    = "Model explanatory power",
    subtitle = "Conditional R² across rolling windows",
    x        = expression(R[conditional]^2),
    y        = NULL
  ) +
  theme_sem() +
  theme(
    #strip.text.y       = element_blank(),
    strip.background.y = element_blank()
  )

p_r2
```

```{r export-fig1}
#| include: false   # Suppress output in HTML; figure is saved to disk only

tiff("../output/1.sem_results_distribution.tiff",
     width = 2800, height = 1300, res = 200)

(p_r2 | p_estimates) +
  plot_layout(widths = c(1, 3)) +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Comparing the relative influence of soil microclimate and root dynamics under different management intensities",
    theme = theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold"))
  )

invisible(dev.off())
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 3. Deterministic vs. stochastic processes

> Are observed causal effects statistically distinguishable from random noise?

A path coefficient is considered **deterministic** (ecologically meaningful) if its observed value substantially exceeds what would be expected under random permutation. This is assessed by a z-score relative to the null distribution:

$$z = \frac{\hat{\beta}_{\text{obs}} - \mu_{\text{null}}}{\sigma_{\text{null}}}$$

Values beyond \|z\| = 1.96 indicate that the observed effect exceeds the 95 % null interval.

```{r z-score-prep}
# Z-score: number of null SDs separating the observed estimate from the null mean
# Filter for observed results and calculate Z-score using existing null columns
zscore_results <- data_sem_results %>%
  filter(type == "observed", p_value < 0.05) %>%
  mutate(
    # Use the columns visible in your View() output
    z_score = (std_estimate - null_mean_estimate) / null_sd_estimate
  ) %>%
  # Corrected Outlier Filter: Use 0.75 for the upper bound
  filter(
    z_score >= quantile(z_score, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE) - 3 * IQR(z_score, na.rm = TRUE),
    z_score <= quantile(z_score, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE) + 3 * IQR(z_score, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
```

```{r plot-zscore-histogram}
#| fig-cap: >
#|   Histogram of z-scores for all observed path coefficients (p < 0.05)
#|   relative to the permutation null distribution. Dashed red lines mark
#|   the 95 % null interval (|z| = 1.96). Coefficients outside this interval
#|   cannot be attributed to chance alone.
#| fig-alt: >
#|   Histogram of z-scores with reference lines at ±1.96.
#| fig-width:  6
#| fig-height: 4

zscore_results %>%
  ggplot(aes(z_score)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = SEM_COLORS$position[["A"]], alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(-1.96, 1.96),
             linetype = "dashed", color = SEM_COLORS$null_line) +
  labs(
    title    = "Z-score distribution vs. permutation null",
    subtitle = "Dashed lines: 95 % null interval (|z| = 1.96)",
    x        = "Z-score",
    y        = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_sem()
```

```{r plot-zscore-by-path}
#| fig-cap: >
#|   Boxplots of z-scores by management position and causal path. Dashed lines
#|   mark |z| = 1.96. Paths whose median z-score consistently exceeds this
#|   threshold exhibit non-random, ecologically meaningful environmental forcing.
#| fig-alt: >
#|   Faceted boxplots of z-scores split by management position and causal path.
#| fig-width:  12
#| fig-height: 4

zscore_results %>%
  # Ensure path_id is still a factor for consistent ordering
  mutate(path_id = factor(path_id, levels = path_levels$path_id)) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(z_score, position, fill = position)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(-1.96, 1.96), 
             linetype = "dashed", color = SEM_COLORS$null_line) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.5, outlier.shape = NA) + # 'outliers = FALSE' is deprecated in newer ggplot2
  scale_fill_manual(values = position_palette) +
  # Use label_wrap_gen to prevent long path names from overlapping
  facet_grid(. ~ path_id, scales = "free", labeller = label_wrap_gen(width = 15)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Z-scores by management position and causal path",
    subtitle = "Each box aggregates all significant windows for that path",
    x        = "Z-score",
    y        = "Position"
  ) +
  theme_sem() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

```{r plot-zscore-over-time}
#| fig-cap: >
#|   Monthly evolution of z-scores, split by causal path and management
#|   position. Each box represents the distribution of window-level z-scores
#|   within a calendar month. Dashed lines mark |z| = 1.96. Temporal patterns
#|   in z-score magnitude may reveal seasonally varying ecological forcing.
#| fig-alt: >
#|   Faceted boxplots of monthly z-score distributions, coloured by position.
#| fig-width:  12
#| fig-height: 8

zscore_results %>%
  mutate(
    path_id = str_replace(path_id, "microclimate_index_1", microclimate_index[1]),
    path_id = str_replace(path_id, "microclimate_index_2", microclimate_index[2])
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(
    x    = as.factor(floor_date(as.Date(date_start), unit = "month")),
    y    = z_score,
    fill = position
  )) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(-1.96, 1.96),
             linetype = "dashed", color = SEM_COLORS$null_line) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.5, position = position_dodge()) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = position_palette) +
  facet_grid(path_id ~ ., scales = "free",
                 labeller = labeller(
      path_id  = label_wrap_gen(width = 15))) +
  labs(
    title    = "Monthly evolution of z-scores",
    subtitle = "By causal path and management position",
    x        = "Month",
    y        = "Z-score",
    fill     = "Position"
  ) +
  theme_sem(legend_pos = "bottom") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
```

> 💡 **Open questions:**
>
> -   Is CV the best indicator of temporal stability, or should we consider alternatives (e.g. local entropy, regime shift detection)?
> -   Should only pre-filtered significant relationships be included in the CV computation?
> -   Could abrupt transitions in z-score time series reveal ecological regime shifts?

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 4. Temporal stability of causal paths

> Do the strengths of causal paths fluctuate over time, or remain steady?

The **Coefficient of Variation (CV)** of standardised path coefficients across rolling windows distinguishes:

-   **Low CV** → "steady" forcing: the environment has a predictable, consistent impact
-   **High CV** → "pulsed" or unstable forcing: the relationship fluctuates over time

Comparing CV across management positions tests whether human intensification destabilises the ecosystem by replacing natural, steady rhythms with erratic biological responses.

```{r cv-analysis}
#| fig-cap: >
#|   Lollipop chart of the temporal coefficient of variation (CV) of
#|   standardised path coefficients, stratified by management position.
#|   CV = (SD / (|mean| + 0.01)) × 100; the small constant avoids division by
#|   zero when the mean coefficient is near zero. Only paths with at least 10
#|   valid windows are shown. Higher CV indicates more temporally unstable
#|   (pulsed) ecological forcing.
#| fig-alt: >
#|   Horizontal lollipop chart comparing CV of path coefficients between
#|   unmanaged and managed systems.
#| fig-width:  8
#| fig-height: 5

cv_global <- data_sem_results %>%
  filter(taxon != "null model") %>%
  group_by(position, path_id) %>%
  summarise(
    n_windows = n(),
    mean_beta = mean(std_estimate, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_beta   = sd(std_estimate,   na.rm = TRUE),
    # +0.01 avoids division by zero when mean_beta ≈ 0
    cv_beta   = (sd_beta / (abs(mean_beta) + 0.01)) * 100,
    .groups   = "drop"
  ) %>%
  filter(n_windows >= 10)

ggplot(cv_global,
       aes(x = reorder(path_id, cv_beta), y = cv_beta, color = position)) +
  geom_linerange(
    aes(ymin = 0, ymax = cv_beta),
    position  = position_dodge(width = 0.6),
    linewidth = 1, alpha = 0.4
  ) +
  geom_point(position = position_dodge(width = 0.6), size = 3) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_color_manual(values = position_palette) +
  labs(
    title    = "Temporal stability of causal forcing rules",
    subtitle = "Higher CV indicates pulsed or unstable forcing dynamics",
    x        = "SEM pathway",
    y        = "Coefficient of variation (%)",
    color    = "Management position"
  ) +
  theme_sem(legend_pos = "bottom") +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    axis.text.y        = element_text(size = 10)
  )
```

> 💡 **Open questions:**
>
> -   Generate CV stratified by additional factors (season, depth) to enable formal comparison tests?
> -   Should only pre-filtered significant relationships feed into the CV computation?
> -   Could identifying abrupt temporal transitions complement the CV approach?

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 5. Spatial synchrony of forcing

> Are the same causal rules operating simultaneously in both management zones?
>
> When a microclimate variable becomes a strong driver in the unmanaged zone (A), does it simultaneously become a strong driver in the managed zone (C)?

-   **High correlation (r ≈ 1) :** management has not altered the timing of the causal rule — forcing remains governed by regional climate.
-   **Low / no correlation (r ≈ 0) :** management has decoupled the site from the natural rhythm — the rules operating in the managed zone are now independent of the unmanaged ones.

### 5.1 Global synchrony index

```{r sync-global}
#| fig-cap: >
#|   Lollipop chart of the global synchrony index (Pearson r) between
#|   standardised path coefficients in the unmanaged (A) and managed (C)
#|   positions across all time windows. Blue encodes positive synchrony
#|   (coordinated forcing); red encodes antagonistic dynamics. Each panel
#|   shows results for one invertebrate taxon.
#| fig-alt: >
#|   Horizontal lollipop chart of synchrony r values per pathway and taxon,
#|   coloured by synchrony direction.
#| fig-width:  10
#| fig-height: 5

sync_results <- data_sem_results %>%
  select(date_start, position, taxon, path_id, std_estimate) %>% 
  pivot_wider(names_from = position, values_from = std_estimate) %>%
  group_by(path_id, taxon) %>%
  summarise(
    r_A_vs_C = cor(A, C, use = "pairwise.complete.obs"),
    n_obs    = n(),
    .groups  = "drop"
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(r_A_vs_C))

ggplot(sync_results,
       aes(x = reorder(path_id, r_A_vs_C), y = r_A_vs_C)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0,
             linetype = "dotted", color = SEM_COLORS$sync_anchor, linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_segment(
    aes(xend = reorder(path_id, r_A_vs_C), y = 0, yend = r_A_vs_C),
    color = SEM_COLORS$segment, linewidth = 1
  ) +
  geom_point(aes(color = r_A_vs_C), size = 5) +
  scale_color_gradient2(
    low      = SEM_COLORS$sync_low,
    mid      = SEM_COLORS$sync_mid,
    high     = SEM_COLORS$sync_high,
    midpoint = 0
  ) +
  coord_flip() +
  facet_wrap(~ taxon) +
  labs(
    title = "Ecological decoupling: global synchrony of path coefficients (A vs. C)",
    x     = "Forcing pathway",
    y     = "Synchrony index (r)",
    color = "Synchrony"
  ) +
  theme_sem(legend_pos = "right") +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    axis.text.y        = element_text(face = "bold")
  )
```

### 5.2 Rolling synchrony over time

```{r sync-rolling}
#| fig-cap: >
#|   Temporal dynamics of the rolling synchrony index between management
#|   positions A and C. Each line represents one invertebrate taxon; the curve
#|   is a LOESS smoother (span = 0.2) to reduce window-to-window noise. The rug
#|   at the bottom of each panel indicates data density over time. Periods of
#|   negative r indicate that the same causal pathway operates in opposing
#|   directions in the two management zones simultaneously.
#| fig-alt: >
#|   Faceted line plots of rolling Pearson r over time, one panel per causal
#|   pathway, coloured by taxon.
#| fig-width:  12
#| fig-height: 7

#' Compute rolling Pearson correlation between positions A and C
#'
#' @param df          Dataframe with columns `day`, `A`, `C`.
#' @param window_size Integer. Rolling window width (number of observations).
#' @return Input dataframe with an added `roll_cor` column.
calculate_rolling_sync <- function(df, window_size = 21) {
  df %>%
    arrange(date_start) %>%
    mutate(roll_cor = slider::slide2_dbl(
      A, C,
      ~ cor(.x, .y, use = "pairwise.complete.obs"),
      .before = window_size
    ))
}

# Wide-format: A and C as separate columns for correlation computation
position_sync_binary <- data_sem_results %>%
  mutate(
    path_id = paste(predictor, "->", response),
    path_id = str_replace(path_id, "microclimate_index_1", microclimate_index[1]),
    path_id = str_replace(path_id, "microclimate_index_2", microclimate_index[2])
  ) %>%
  select(date_start, position, taxon, path_id, estimate) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = position, values_from = estimate)

# Apply rolling correlation within each path × taxon group
rolling_sync_data <- position_sync_binary %>%
  group_by(path_id, taxon) %>%
  nest() %>%
  mutate(rolling_cor = map(data, calculate_rolling_sync)) %>%
  select(-data) %>%
  unnest(rolling_cor)

rolling_sync_data %>%
  filter(!is.na(roll_cor), !path_id %in% c('temp_soil_max -> root','hum_soil_max -> root' )) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = date_start, y = roll_cor, group = taxon, color = taxon)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0,
             linetype = "dashed", color = SEM_COLORS$null_line, alpha = 0.5) +
  # LOESS smoothing (span = 0.2) reduces window-to-window noise
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", span = 0.2, se = FALSE, linewidth = 1) +
  geom_rug(alpha = 0.1) +
  facet_grid(path_id~. , scales = "free") +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(
    title    = "Temporal dynamics of ecological synchrony (A vs. C)",
    subtitle = "Positive r: coordinated forcing | Negative r: antagonistic forcing",
    x        = "Date",
    y        = "Rolling correlation (r)",
    color    = "Taxon"
  ) +
  theme_sem(legend_pos = "bottom") +
  theme(
    strip.text  = element_text(size = 7, face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x = element_blank()
  )
```
